title: THE SONG OF THE SILVER THRONE
blurb: A stolen anthem. A stolen throne. A voice rising from the slums to shatter the lies of the elite. Every note she sings could reclaim her kingdom—or get her killed before the Twin Moons align.
comparable: For fans of *The Lies of Locke Lamora* by Scott Lynch, *The Jasmine Throne* by Tasha Suri, and *The Name of the Wind* by Patrick Rothfuss

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The Hall of Harmonics shimmered under chandeliers of blown glass and silver wire. Sylphine stood alone on the polished dais, fingers hovering above the keyboard like a ghost about to touch a wound. The audience—draped in silks the color of spilled ink—leaned forward as one, breath held, expectation coiling tight.

She did not speak. Could not.

But the piano did.

Her hands descended. The opening arpeggio of *The Silver Lullaby* spilled into the vaulted hall, bright and precise, every note ringing through the throat of the instrument before bleeding into the air. The Conservatory’s finest musicians watched her with admiration, then envy. The patrons murmured, fingers tapping rhythms only they could hear. Maestros Veyla sat stiff-backed in the first tier, lace cuffs framing wrists that had never known a hard day’s labor.

Sylphine’s silence was the performance—a mute girl making music with her body, not her voice. That was the trick. That was the mystery.

Her sleeves rode up as she played, revealing forearms marked with fine scars from years of scraping against strings and keys. One sleeve rode higher than the other tonight. Just enough.

A whisper rippled through the first row. Orsino Mier leaned toward Veyla, jaw tight. “Did you see that?”

Veyla didn’t turn. “See what?”

“Her sleeve. It shifted.”

“Static electricity,” Veyla said. “Or imagination.”

Sylphine reached the second verse. The melody darkened, deeper, more resonant. Some listeners shifted in their seats. Too heavy. Too personal. A lullaby wasn’t supposed to ache.

Then the piano began to glow.

Not the keys. Not the frame. The sheet music beneath her hands—faintly, as if lit from behind—burned with silver light. The audience gasped. Murmurs flared. Veyla stood.

“A trick of the lanterns,” she declared, though her voice wavered.

Sylphine kept playing.

The light pulsed in time with the music. Faster. Brighter. The Conservatory’s great chandeliers dimmed in response, as if the song were drawing power from the hall itself. A child in the back row began to cry.

Veyla’s face darkened. She strode onto the dais, heels sharp against the wood. “Enough.”

Sylphine’s fingers stilled. Silence fell so fast it hurt the ears.

Veyla reached for the sheet music. “Let me see.”

Sylphine pulled her hands away.

Veyla didn’t take no for an answer. She seized the score.

The moment her fingers brushed the paper, the light flared white-hot. A collective cry rose from the audience. Veyla recoiled, clutching a singed hem. The music on the page revealed itself in full—no longer *The Silver Lullaby* but something older, something written in a hand she did not know.

Her breath hitched.

The melody was hers. But the handwriting was not.

Orsino stood abruptly. His chair screeched. “Maestros—”

Veyla tore the page in half. Then again. The fragments fluttered to the floor like dying moths. “A forgery,” she announced, voice trembling but firm. “The work of a charlatan. Sylphine may play what she’s given, but she does not *create*.”

The hall erupted. Some applauded. Others hissed. A few laughed.

Sylphine watched her mentor with hollow eyes. She said nothing.

The performance was over.

She rose and bowed—once, fluid, perfect. Then she turned and walked down the side aisle, her shadow stretching long behind her in the dying light.

In the third tier, a man with a lean face and a silver pin at his lapel watched her go. Kaelis Vire. He held a violin between his knees, though he hadn’t played a note. His fingers tapped the wood, restless, as if she’d left a rhythm in the air he couldn’t ignore.

Behind her, she felt the weight of the Conservatory’s gaze. Ahead, the doors to the Garden of Silent Strings stood open, moonlight spilling across the threshold like a promise.

She stepped into the garden.

The strings of a hundred broken instruments hung limp from the branches of an ancient weeping willow. Sylphine walked beneath them, bare feet brushing petals fallen onto stone. She knelt beside the oldest instrument—a lute with a cracked body, its strings long since broken.

She pressed her palm to the wood.

A single drop of blood welled from the scar on her forearm.

It fell.

Landed on the sheet music still clutched in her sleeve.

The note shimmered. Expanded. Spread.

And beneath the shattered lullaby, another song began to breathe.

Chapter 2

The Grand Duet was always a spectacle. The Conservatory’s elite took their seats in the Hall of Harmonics, their silk gloves snapping open, their fans fanning languidly. Sylphine stood at the center of the stage, her violin resting against her collarbone. The air smelled of polished wood and expensive perfume. Backstage, the other performers whispered behind their hands.

Maestros Veyla ascended the steps to the dais, her crimson robes dragging slightly on the marble. The applause swelled. She raised a hand, and it died instantly.

“Tonight,” she said, “we honor the voice of our age—Sylphine.”

Sylphine bowed. The audience clapped. Veyla waited until the noise subsided.

“Tonight, we hear again her anthem to the Silver Throne. The song of our future. The song of *loyalty*.”

A ripple of approval. Sylphine lifted her bow. The first notes filled the hall, pure and bright, lifting like incense. The Conservatory’s patrons leaned forward, enchanted.

Halfway through the third movement, Veyla stepped forward. Her smile never wavered.

“A moment,” she said.

The music faltered. Sylphine’s fingers paused mid-stroke.

Veyla reached up and plucked the sheet music from the stand.

The bow slipped from Sylphine’s fingers. She didn’t catch it.

Veyla held the page aloft. “This work—this so-called anthem—” Her voice dripped with mock pity. “Is it truly yours?”

Silence.

Sylphine said nothing. She didn’t move. The audience leaned in, breath held.

“Look closely,” Veyla said, tapping the parchment. “The ink is fresh. The hand is unsteady. The composition lacks the maturity of a true masterpiece.” She lowered her voice, as if sharing a confidence. “And the signature—where is the signature?”

She crumpled the corner of the sheet between her fingers. Sylphine’s breath caught. That wasn’t part of the act. That wasn’t staged.

Veyla’s eyes gleamed. “This is plagiarism. A fraud. A mute girl, playing at genius.”

The hall erupted. Some gasped. Others laughed. A patron in the third tier stood, clapping slowly. “Bravo,” he called. “Bravo!”

Sylphine’s hands stayed at her sides. Her face remained blank. But her pulse hammered in her throat.

Orsino stood in the shadows near the wings, his ledger open, his pen hovering over the page. His jaw was tight. He didn’t look at her.

Kaelis Vire watched from the back row, arms crossed, his dark coat blending into the velvet seats. His expression was unreadable. But his fingers drummed against his forearm—once, twice—a rhythm that didn’t match the music.

Veyla let the crumpled sheet drift to the floor. It fluttered like a dying moth.

“Remove her,” she said.

The stagehands moved forward. Sylphine stepped back. She didn’t resist. She didn’t speak. She simply walked off the stage, her black dress whispering against the floor.

Behind her, the music restarted. A different solo. A distraction.

Orsino closed his ledger. His pen snapped.

Kaelis didn’t clap. He didn’t stand. He just watched Sylphine disappear through the side arch.

She didn’t look back.

The slums were quiet at dusk. Crates stacked like broken teeth lined the narrow alley. Sylphine’s room was a cubby above a tannery. The stench of brine and leather seeped through the floorboards. She pushed open the door. Inside, the single candle sputtered. A broken lute lay on the table, its neck cracked.

She sat. Picked up a file. Began to work.

Down the hall, a child’s cough echoed. Light footsteps. A girl—eight, maybe nine—peeked around the doorframe. Her dress was patched, her eyes wide.

“You’re the mute singer,” she said.

Sylphine didn’t look up. She ran a finger along the lute’s broken edge.

“They say you lied,” the girl went on. “They say you stole your song.”

Sylphine picked up a tuning peg. Began to twist.

The girl stepped inside. “My mother says the Conservatory burns people who lie.”

Sylphine’s hands stilled. The peg slipped from her fingers.

She hummed—a single note, low and mournful. The lute’s cracked wood trembled. A string snapped inward, like a sliver of light.

The girl gasped.

Sylphine ignored her. She reached for another string. Plucked it. The note hung in the air, faintly glowing.

The girl backed toward the door. “You’re magic,” she whispered.

Sylphine didn’t answer. She closed her eyes. The melody rose in her mind—clear, unbroken. The anthem. The real one. The one she’d composed years ago, in the Garden of Silent Strings, before she knew what it meant.

Her fingers moved without thought. She began to reconstruct.

The lute’s strings hummed in response, vibrating in sympathy. The glow intensified, painting her hands in silver light.

The girl bolted.

Sylphine didn’t stop.

Chapter 3

The tannery’s stench clung to the walls. Sylphine worked by candlelight, her breath steady, her hands precise. The broken lute lay before her, its cracked neck splayed like a broken spine. She picked up a chisel. Tapped gently along the fracture.

The slum child had gone. The alley was quiet. Only the distant drip of water and the murmur of voices from the market square below broke the stillness.

She didn’t hear the footsteps.

A shadow fell across the table.

Sylphine didn’t look up. Her fingers brushed the chisel’s tip against the wood, testing the grain.

“You’re still here.”

The man stepped closer. His boots were scuffed, his coat too fine for this place. Orsino Mier. He carried a leather case, worn at the edges. The Vault of Unwritten Scores stamped in faded ink.

She set the chisel down.

Orsino exhaled. His breath smelled of wine. “I thought you’d be gone by now. Past time.”

Sylphine picked up a tuning hammer. Placed it deliberately on the table between them.

“You’ve got no future in this hole,” he said. “The Conservatory’s forgotten you. The city whispers your name with shame. Even the slums won’t keep you when winter comes.”

She didn’t react. Her gaze stayed on the lute.

Orsino opened his case. Inside, a stack of charred paper. Ash curled from the edges. He tapped the pile with one finger. “I burned it. All of it. The original anthem. The drafts. Every trace.”

Sylphine’s hand twitched. Just once.

Orsino closed the case with a snap. “You have no proof. No song. No power. It’s over.”

She reached for the hammer. Lifted it.

“Don’t,” he said. “It won’t bring it back.”

Sylphine tapped the hammer’s head against the table. Once. Twice. The sound was sharp, final.

Orsino shifted his weight. “Veyla thinks she’s won. The Conservatory believes in her. The anthem plays tonight in the Hall of Harmonics. She conducts it herself. Claims it as her own.”

Sylphine’s fingers stilled.

“She stood on the dais,” he went on, voice dropping. “Smiled. Let them cheer. Called it *her* masterpiece. *Her* legacy.” He laughed, short and bitter. “Funny how history bends to the loudest voice.”

Sylphine picked up the lute’s neck. Ran her thumb along the fracture. The wood shimmered faintly. A thread of light pulsed beneath the surface.

Orsino didn’t see it. He was watching her hands. “You’re wasting time. I gave you a week’s wages. Enough to leave the city. Start somewhere new. But you won’t go.”

She set the neck down. Reached for a string.

“What are you doing?” he asked.

She picked up a fresh string. Threaded it through the tuning peg. Began to wind.

“Stop it,” Orsino said. He grabbed her wrist. His fingers dug in. “You can’t reconstruct it from memory. It’s impossible. I know—”

She yanked her arm free. The string snapped. It whipped through the air, leaving a silver arc in the candlelight.

Orsino flinched. “—I know the risks.”

Sylphine ignored him. She picked up another string. Tested its tension between her fingers. The note it would make was already forming in her mind.

Orsino exhaled through his nose. “You’re making a mistake.”

She began to hum. Low. Soft. A thread of melody, barely audible. The lute responded. The broken edges of the neck pulsed with light. The strings thrummed in sympathy, though none were tuned.

Orsino’s face darkened. “That’s not how it works. You can’t just—”

She hummed louder. The melody grew. The light spread. It pooled on the table like mercury, shifting, reforming. The lute’s cracks sealed themselves. The wood smoothed. The fractures faded.

Her fingers moved without thought. She pressed the string into the nut. Adjusted the peg. Tested the pitch.

The note rang out—clear, strong, impossible.

Orsino’s breath hitched. “No. No, that’s not—”

The candle flame stretched. The light from the lute brightened. The air hummed. The melody deepened, resonated. It wasn’t just sound anymore. It was presence. Weight.

Orsino staggered back. His boot caught the case. It tipped. The charred papers spilled. Ashes drifted. Some landed on the table. Others scattered across the floor.

The melody swelled. Sylphine’s eyes were closed. Her lips moved silently. She was lost in it. Rebuilding. Remembering. Reclaiming.

The light from the lute surged. It spilled over the table. Over Orsino’s feet. His coat. The ashes trembled. Then, one by one, they lifted. They danced. They rearranged themselves in the air.

A shape formed. A spiral. A signature.

Orsino’s hands shook. “You can’t,” he whispered. “You don’t understand what you’re doing.”

Sylphine opened her eyes. The lute glowed in her hands. The strings vibrated. The melody hung between them, undeniable.

Orsino took a step back. His heel crushed a charred scrap of paper. The fire in the grate flared.

“It’s not just a song,” she said. Her voice was rough. Unused. But the words were clear.

Orsino shook his head. “It’s plagiarism. Forgery. A lie.”

The lute’s light pulsed. The melody resonated in his bones. He felt it. Deep down. Like a chord he’d never heard but always known.

Orsino’s knees buckled. He grabbed the edge of the table. His fingers curled into the wood.

“It’s not about creation,” Sylphine said. “It’s about blood.”

The candle guttered. The room fell into near-darkness. Only the lute’s glow remained. Silver threads of light wove between them, connecting her to the instrument, to the room, to Orsino.

He looked up. His face was pale. “What did you do?”

The lute’s strings hummed. The melody swelled. Sylphine’s fingers danced. She was reconstructing more than wood and wire. She was rebuilding her life. Her name. Her truth.

Orsino’s breath came fast. “You’re not Sylphine,” he said. “You’re Seraphine. The Vaelith heir.”

The words hung in the air. Final. Unchangeable.

The lute’s glow flickered. Then steadied. Brightened.

Orsino’s hands went to his coat. He fumbled with the buttons. His ledger was inside. The one he’d brought. The one he’d never opened.

Sylphine didn’t stop playing. The melody rose. Filled the room. Filled the silence.

Orsino’s breath hitched. He pulled the ledger free. Flipped it open. Pages fluttered. Then stilled.

On the flyleaf, in ink she didn’t recognize, was a single line:

*I am Seraphine Vaelith. The bloodline sings true.*

Orsino’s fingers trembled. “How—?”

The lute’s light pulsed. The melody resonated. Sylphine looked at him. Her eyes were clear. Her face was calm.

She played on.

Orsino’s breath came in shallow gasps. He snapped the ledger shut. Stuffed it into his coat. “You’re playing with fire,” he said.

Sylphine didn’t answer. Her fingers moved. The melody deepened. The room filled with sound.

Orsino turned. He lurched toward the door. His coat brushed the table. The lute’s glow flared, just for a second, like a warning.

He didn’t look back.

The door slammed.

Sylphine kept playing. The melody filled the silence. The lute’s light painted the walls. The shadows danced. The room felt alive.

She closed her eyes. The music wrapped around her. The anthem. The real one. The one she’d composed in blood and bone.

Her fingers moved on their own. The melody rose. Strengthened. Glowed brighter.

A single note hung in the air. Long. Clear. Silver.

It didn’t fade.

It pulsed. Like a heartbeat.

Chapter 4

The Hall of Harmonics smelled of candle wax and damp velvet. Maestros Veyla stood beneath the gilded proscenium, bathed in the honeyed glow of twenty chandeliers, her gown a cascade of silver thread that caught the light like running water. The Conservatory’s elite filled the velvet tiers, their murmurs a current of admiration, envy, and the occasional dropped fork.

Sylphine watched from the back, just inside the arched doorway. Not invited. Not even acknowledged. The sight of Veyla beneath the silver canopy, raising her arms to conduct the first notes of the anthem she’d stolen, made her fingers curl.

Orsino stood to Veyla’s left, holding the polished decanter with the precision of a man who had learned years ago how to pour without trembling. His eyes flicked once toward the shadowed doorway—Sylphine’s doorway—and then away, as if he had seen nothing at all.

A servant brushed past her, murmuring, “The slums say her name is whispered again.” The words skated past, unheard.

Sylphine didn’t react. She watched Veyla’s baton slice the air, watched the chorus sway, their voices rising in chorus—*her* anthem, sung with someone else’s breath. Her nails dug into her palms. The pain grounded her.

A gloved hand settled on her shoulder.

She turned.

Kaelis Vire stood there, his coat darker than the crowd, his smile sharp enough to cut. His fingers were warm. Too warm. Familiar.

“You’re late,” he said. “Or early. I can never tell with you.”

She stepped aside. His hand dropped.

“You’re not eating,” he observed. “No wine. No toast. Just standing like a ghost at a feast.”

She didn’t answer. The anthem swelled. She could feel the vibrations in her bones.

Kaelis leaned closer. His voice dropped. “Veyla’s flush with victory tonight. She’s announced a grand Edict tomorrow—binding the minor houses in loyalty. Claims the anthem gave her the right.” He chuckled. “Funny, that. The anthem was always hers.”

Sylphine’s gaze flicked to him. Not a question. Not a betrayal. Just observation.

Kaelis caught the look. “You don’t trust me.”

She tilted her head. One slow motion. Denial wasn’t worth the breath.

“Good,” he said. “You shouldn’t.”

He reached into his coat. Pulled out a folded paper. A musical score, yellowed at the edges. He held it just out of reach.

“You want this,” he said. “I know you do.”

She didn’t move.

“It’s not the anthem,” he said. “But it’s yours. Or was. The late-night drafts. The scratches in the margins. Your handwriting. I found it in the Vault of Unwritten Scores.”

Her fingers twitched.

Kaelis watched them. “I could give it to you. Tonight. For a price.”

The anthem crescendoed. Silver threads of sound wove through the air. Sylphine could almost see them—threads of light, weaving between Veyla and the crowd, binding them all.

“Name it,” she said. Her voice was rough. Unused. But the words came.

Kaelis smiled. “I want in. Not as your enemy. As your ally. You’re going to take the throne. I can help. But I need to understand the song. All of it.”

The anthem rose. The candles flared.

Sylphine looked at the paper. Then at him. “No.”

Kaelis didn’t flinch. “No to the paper? Or no to me?”

She stepped closer. Took the paper. Unfolded it. The melody was familiar—fragmented, incomplete. A sketch of something she’d once dreamed. She could almost hear the missing notes.

Kaelis watched her face. “You recognize it.”

She folded it again

Chapter 5

The Hall of Harmonics was too warm.

Veyla felt the heat crawl up her spine as she stood beneath the silver canopy, her voice rising above the gathered patrons. The chandeliers dripped wax onto the polished floor. She could taste copper on her tongue. *Always the same. Always too close.*

Her arms ached. The Edict sprawled across the page—a melody of binding, of obligation, of *loyalty*. She had sung it three times before tonight. Each rendition stronger. Each response from the chorus sweeter. Tonight it would hold. Tonight it would *last*.

Below her, the nobles shifted in their velvet seats. Some clutched goblets. Others fanned themselves with stiff parchment. All of them listening. All of them waiting for her to falter.

She would not.

A flicker in the corner of her vision—Orsino, standing motionless beside the decanter, his expression unreadable. He held the decanter correctly. Perfectly. But his knuckles were white.

Veyla ignored him.

She raised her baton higher.

The chorus inhaled in unison. Their voices lifted, rich and resonant, weaving the Edict into the air. The notes curled around the columns, pressed against the frescoed ceiling. The melody was hers now. The authority was hers. The *future* was hers.

Then her voice cracked.

A thin, reedy sound—like a child’s when the song grew too long. The hall inhaled as one. Veyla’s pulse roared in her skull. She swallowed, forced another note. The chorus faltered. Their eyes flickered toward her, confused.

She wasn’t supposed to falter.

A page in the back, no older than twelve, dropped a tray. Silver clattered against stone. The sound echoed, sharp and sudden. The patrons flinched.

Veyla steadied herself. Swallowed again. The Edict trembled in her hands.

She would not let it fall.

Behind her, the arches leading to the Garden of Silent Strings stood open. A draft whispered through, carrying the scent of night-blooming jasmine and damp earth. She could not see the garden from here. She did not need to.

But something was wrong.

The air smelled of copper again. Not the wax. Not the wine. Something older. Something *wrong*.

She pressed on.

The chorus recovered, their voices thickening, pushing the melody forward. Veyla found her footing. Her voice steadied. She was almost there. Almost—

A single note echoed from the garden.

A lute. Her lute. The one she had thrown away years ago when the girl became a liability.

The lute began to play.

The Edict stuttered in her grip. The chorus faltered mid-phrase. Patrons turned toward the garden, their murmurs rising like a tide. Veyla’s skin burned.

The lute’s notes were clear. Precise. *Her* notes.

She had composed them. Of course she had. But the lute had been silent. Cast aside. Buried beneath years of neglect.

Unless—

Unless it remembered.

Veyla’s vision narrowed. The garden, the lute, the traitorous instrument—all of it coalesced into a single point of failure. Her Edict was failing. The lute was playing. The chorus was watching her. Orsino was watching her. The entire Conservatory was watching her.

And the lute’s song was growing louder.

She could not let it.

“Enough!” Veyla’s voice rang out, raw and unchecked. The chorus fell silent. The patrons froze. The lute, somehow, did not.

It played on.

Veyla dropped the baton. The Edict fluttered to the floor. She did not care. She strode toward the garden, her silver-threaded gown whispering against the stone. The lute’s notes followed her, insistent. Mocking.

She reached the arch. The garden lay beyond, bathed in silver moonlight filtered through the Conservatory’s glass dome. Vines curled over stone benches. The old lute sat on the bench where she had left it—propped against the stone, its strings glowing faintly, as if lit from within.

Veyla stepped forward.

The lute’s strings vibrated. A single, clear note hung in the air. Not the anthem. Not the Edict. Something older. Something *familiar*.

It was the Vaelith signature.

The lute began to play *her* anthem.

Veyla’s breath stopped.

The strings snapped mid-note.

Silence.

Then—footsteps on stone.

A figure emerged from the shadows between the cypress trees, coat dark, hands raised slightly in surrender.

Kaelis Vire stepped into the moonlight.

His eyes flickered to the lute. To Veyla. To the broken strings.

“Maestros Duskwhisper,” he said, voice smooth. “I believe your Edict is finished.”

Chapter 6

The Vault of Unwritten Scores smelled of parchment and damp.

Sylphine crouched behind a stack of folios bound in black morocco, her breath shallow. The air here never quite dried—too many precious things stored too close together. She adjusted the stolen ledger in her grip. It was heavy with names, dates, and the Conservatory’s secret dealings. Not the coin she’d hoped for, but it would do.

A shadow passed over the aisle.

She froze.

Footsteps. Slow. Deliberate. Like someone testing the weight of silence itself.

Sylphine slid the ledger under her coat and pressed herself into the gloom between two shelves. The steps stopped. She counted her pulse. One. Two. Three.

A hand snaked around the shelf.

A man’s voice, rough with slum habit, said, “I don’t see nothin’, friend.”

Sylphine exhaled. Not Orsino. Not the guards.

She stepped forward, ledger held just enough into the dim light to catch the man’s eye.

He was lean, his face scored with old burns, his coat patched with scavenged velvet. A Vex informant—one of those who thrived in the cracks between the Conservatory and the slums.

“Book for ink,” she signed.

The man’s eyes flicked over the ledger. Then to her face. His smile didn’t reach his scars.

“You ain’t Conservatory,” he said. “You ain’t slum. You something in between.”

Sylphine tapped the ledger.

He shrugged. “Twenty vials. Luminous. Fresh.”

She nodded.

He held out a glass vial stoppered with wax. When she reached for it, he didn’t let go.

“What’s in it for me?” he asked.

She reached into her coat again. Pulled out a Conservatory patron’s signet ring. Not valuable here. But it had weight.

He examined the crest. Nodded once. Released the vial.

She pressed the ledger into his hands. He weighed it, satisfied, then melted back into the stacks.

Sylphine uncorked the vial. The luminous ink inside moved like trapped moonlight. She dabbed her fingertip. Cold. Vibrant. Alive.

She had one chance.

Orsino’s desk was at the back of the vault, under the stained-glass window that threw motley light onto the parchment. She moved quickly, stepping over loose sheets, avoiding the creaks in the floorboards. The ledger went onto his blotter. Not under it—on top. A message. A record. A provocation.

Then she was gone, her boots silent on the worn rug.

---

Orsino arrived an hour later.

He paused at the vault door, as he always did, to adjust his collar and smooth his sleeves. The habit of performance clung to him even in emptiness. He stepped inside, his lantern casting long shadows.

He saw the ledger first.

His eyebrows lifted. He moved toward his desk.

Then he saw the ink.

It wasn’t just on the ledger. It had spilled—no, *flowed*—across the blotter. Tiny, glowing notes. A melody. Half-formed. Flickering.

Orsino set the lantern down.

He didn’t reach for the ledger. He traced the ink with his fingertip.

A single note trembled under his touch.

He froze.

The note repeated. Softly. Insistently.

Orsino knelt.

The ink rose from the parchment like smoke. It curled into a shape—two descending lines, then a curve. A motif. A signature.

His breath caught.

He knew that motif.

He knew that signature.

It was the Vaelith anthem.

The ink pulsed. One note. Two. A phrase.

Orsino’s hands began to shake.

He didn’t believe in ghosts. He didn’t believe in bloodlines. He believed in order. In preservation. In the Conservatory.

But the ink was singing.

Not to him.

To someone else.

He stood abruptly, knocking the lantern. The glass chimney shattered. Flame licked at parchment.

Orsino didn’t move to put it out.

He stared at the spreading fire. At the music forming on the burning page.

The anthem rose into the air, thin and clear, before the smoke swallowed it whole.

Chapter 7

The Hall of Harmonics exhaled in silver and candlelight.

Two hundred guests had gathered beneath the vaulted ceiling, their silk gowns and brocade coats catching the glow of enchanted chandeliers. The air smelled of crushed violets, spilled wine, and the faint metallic tang of polished instruments. Sylphine stood near the archway, her fingers tracing the embroidered edge of her own banner—*Sylphine, Prodigy of the Conservatory*—stitched in thread that shimmered like starlight when she moved.

A man in a ruffled shirt stood on the dais, wine sloshing over the rim of his glass. He raised it high, his voice thick with flirtation and brandy.

“To Sylphine!” he declared. “The voice of our golden age! The hand that wove the anthem that chains the throne to lawful harmony!” He drank deeply. Wine dribbled down his chin. “Without her, we’d still be drowning in dissonance!”

Laughter rippled. A few patrons clapped.

Sylphine’s smile didn’t waver. It never did.

Then she felt the temperature drop.

Maestros Veyla stood beneath the banners of the Conservatory, her gaze a blade angled toward the drunkard. The room stilled. Even the violins paused mid-note.

Veyla lifted one hand. Not to gesture. Not to applaud. To command.

The chatter died.

She inhaled, and the air itself seemed to listen. Her voice emerged not as sound but as presence—cool, precise, inevitable.

“Silence.”

Every instrument in the hall fell still. The strings of the grand piano sagged, muted. A soprano’s breath stalled mid-inhale. The wine glasses stopped trembling.

Sylphine felt the pressure in her own throat. She swallowed hard. The command wasn’t aimed at her. Not yet. But it hovered.

Veyla stepped forward, her black gown whispering over the marble. She stopped before the drunken patron, who blinked, bewildered, as if waking from a dream.

“You mistake homage for truth,” she said. Her voice carried no volume, yet every syllable landed like a stone. “Sylphine is a vessel. But the anthem sings through her only because we allow it.”

The man nodded dumbly. He set down his glass. The clink was loud in the vacuum of sound.

Veyla turned her gaze—not to Sylphine—but over her, through her, as if she were merely part of the architecture.

“Let us honor what lasts,” she said. “Not fleeting voices, but enduring laws.”

The guests nodded. Some clapped again. The violins lifted their bows.

Sylphine felt the pressure lift. Air rushed back into her lungs. She exhaled—silent. Measured.

She met Veyla’s eyes for the first time in months.

Not defiance. Not fear.

Recognition.

Veyla’s lips thinned. She turned away.

Sylphine exhaled fully. The moment had passed.

Then she saw *him*.

A figure stood motionless near the far tapestry—one woven with a mural of the first Conservatory masters. His robes were not of the Conservatory. They were dark, unadorned, the color of old parchment. A hood shadowed his face, but his hands were bare. Long-fingered. Still.

Kaelis Vire.

He was watching her.

Not the stage. Not the anthem. *Her.*

Sylphine didn’t look away. She never did.

A tremor ran through her fingers. She clenched them into fists.

Then she turned and walked toward the balcony doors, letting the crowd swallow her.

The night was cool. The Garden of Silent Strings stretched beyond the glass—rows of harps and lyres strung with silver wire, their frames carved with long-dead names. She stepped onto the dew-slick stones, her breath visible in the chill.

She heard fabric rustle behind her.

Sylphine didn’t turn.

“You’re not supposed to be out here,” she signed without looking.

Kaelis stepped into the moonlight. His hood fell back. His hair was unbound, dark as polished mahogany. His eyes were not silver or gold. They were deep brown, like soil after rain.

“Neither are you,” he said. “Unless you’re planning to sing.”

She turned fully. Her hands moved faster than her voice ever could. “You followed me.”

“I did.”

She crossed her arms. “Why?”

“Because you’re the only one who doesn’t know why.”

The words landed like a plucked string—clear, unexpected, vibrating.

Sylphine stepped closer. “I know exactly why.”

He tilted his head. “Do you?”

She exhaled through her nose. A mistake. She caught the scent of bergamot and old paper—his scent. It made her throat tighten.

“You want the anthem,” she signed. “You told me so.”

“Not the anthem,” he said. “The *truth* in it.”

She went still.

Kaelis stepped forward. His robe whispered against the stone. “The Chorus hasn’t found it yet. But they’ve found *you.*”

She wanted to step back. She didn’t.

“You’re lying,” she signed.

“I’m late,” he said. “That’s the truth.”

She shook her head sharply, once. “No.”

Kaelis reached into his robe. Not for a weapon. For something small, wrapped in cloth.

He unfolded it.

A sheet of music.

Familiar.

Her pulse stuttered.

It was her handwriting. Not the anthem. Something else. A fragment. A chorus. A phrase she’d scribbled in the dark during her first winter in the slums.

Kaelis held it out. “You sang this once. In the Garden. When you were twelve.”

Her breath caught. She didn’t take it.

He lowered his hand. “But you didn’t compose it.”

The air between them shifted.

Sylphine felt the Garden tilt. The wires of the harps hummed faintly, as if sensing her unraveling.

Kaelis’s voice dropped. “That melody is older than the Conservatory. Older than the Vaelith name. It’s the first song a Vaelith heir sings when they wake.”

Her hands trembled.

She did not reach for the paper.

Kaelis’s gaze didn’t waver. “You don’t remember, do you?”

The Garden went silent. Not by command. By realization.

Sylphine’s knees nearly gave.

Kaelis stepped closer. Too close. His voice was a thread pulled taut. “The Chorus has been singing it in the shadows for months. Weaving it into the city’s music. And tonight, it answered back.”

She shook her head. No. No no no.

Kaelis’s hand hovered near her wrist. Not touching. Inviting.

“It called *you,* Sylphine.”

The name fell into the night like a dropped lyre string.

Sylphine’s breath turned to ice in her lungs.

Then she heard it.

A note.

Not from the instruments. Not from the city. From *her.*

A single, clear pitch—rising from her throat like breath through a reed flute.

She clapped a hand over her mouth.

Kaelis’s eyes widened.

The note held. Trembled. Then resolved into a phrase—one she had never sung before, yet knew as if it had always lived inside her.

It was the theme from the fragment he held.

The lost Vaelith lullaby.

She ripped her hand away from her mouth. The note continued. Unbidden. Uncontrolled.

Kaelis took her wrist gently.

She tried to pull free.

He held firm. His voice was low, urgent. “Don’t fight it. Let it sing.”

The Garden trembled. The harps hummed in sympathetic vibration. Dewdrops shivered from the strings.

Sylphine’s voice cracked into harmony. Not one note. Two. Three.

The fragment unfolded into a full melody—ancient, aching, powerful.

It was the anthem.

Not hers.

The *real* one.

Kaelis’s grip tightened. He wasn’t pulling her toward him. He was pulling her *into* the music.

The fragment turned into the anthem.

The anthem turned into *command.*

Sylphine’s voice soared. The air itself bent toward her. The harps’ strings glowed faint silver in the dark.

She wasn’t singing.

She was *remembering.*

And the city would never be the same.

Chapter 8

The courtyard of the Conservatory was a frozen lake of marble and mirrors.

Sunlight fractured across the tiles, splitting into a thousand shards of gold and white. Sylphine stood beneath the portico, watching her shadow stretch long and jagged over the stones. The air smelled of warm stone and crushed jasmine from the vendor’s carts near the gate.

She adjusted the strap of her case, her fingers brushing the worn leather. Inside lay the ledger she’d stolen from the archivist’s vault—proof of Veyla’s lies, names inked in blood-red dye that glowed only under moonlight. Tonight, it would burn.

A soft chord trembled through the air.

Not from the instruments in the practice halls. Not from the wind through the eaves.

It came from the street.

Sylphine froze. The chord resolved into a melody—one she’d never heard before, yet her body knew. Her pulse jumped in time with the rhythm. Her fingers twitched toward an invisible lute.

The courtyard emptied. Not by command. By instinct. Patrons paused mid-step. Musicians lowered their bows. A tenor dropped his sheet music, pages scattering like startled birds.

The melody thickened.

A second voice layered in—dissonant, deliberate. Then a third. The harmony coiled around the first, twisting it into something jagged, something *wrong*. The jasmine scent turned cloying, then sour.

Sylphine’s breath hitched.

The melody wasn’t just wrong.

It was *bleeding*.

Harmony bled into disharmony. Notes seeped into silence. A perfect fifth collapsed into a fourth. A trill dissolved into a groan.

Then the nobles dropped.

A woman in sapphire silk crumpled first, her knees folding like wet paper. A man in a brocade coat gasped, his hands clawing at his throat. His wine glass shattered on the tiles, ruby liquid spreading like a wound.

Sylphine’s case slipped from her fingers. It landed with a dull thud, the lock clicking open. Pages from the ledger fluttered out, glowing faintly in the sunlight.

A hand gripped her shoulder.

She flinched, turning.

Orsino stood behind her, his face ashen. His scholar’s robe was rumpled, his spectacles askew. He held a file of loose sheets—copies, she realized. Of the anthem’s original draft.

His voice was a rasp. “We have to go. Now.”

Sylphine shook her head. She bent to gather the pages, her fingers moving on instinct.

Orsino’s grip tightened. “Don’t you hear it?”

She did.

The melody wasn’t just in the air.

It was in *her bones*.

The dissonance coiled around her ribs, tightening. Her vision blurred at the edges. She forced herself to straighten, to look at Orsino.

His eyes were wide, pupils blown. “The Chorus. They’re singing it into existence. They’ve been weaving it into the city’s music for weeks. And now—”

He didn’t finish.

The courtyard groaned.

Not the stone. The music.

The anthem’s original melody—the one she’d written as a girl, the one Veyla had claimed as her own—suddenly *answered*. The dissonant harmony cracked apart. The notes that had been bleeding reassembled into something pure, something *alive*.

Sylphine’s breath left her.

The anthem was singing *back*.

Orsino staggered. He dropped the sheets. They scattered, the music spilling out like entrails. “It’s the bloodline,” he whispered. “It’s *responding.*”

Sylphine’s hands trembled.

The anthem’s first bars rippled through the courtyard, lifting the fallen nobles as if pulled by invisible strings. Their chests rose. Their eyes fluttered open.

Then they screamed.

Not in pain.

In *recognition*.

It wasn’t the anthem they heard.

It was *their* anthem.

The one they’d been taught to revere, to kneel for, to die for.

Sylphine’s knees nearly gave.

Orsino didn’t wait. He seized her arm, dragging her toward the arched exit. “Veyla can’t know what this means. She’ll burn the Conservatory to the ground before she lets it spread.”

Sylphine yanked free.

She wasn’t running.

She was listening.

The anthem wasn’t just responding to her presence.

It was *choosing* her.

Orsino’s voice was a blade. “Do you want to die here? Or do you want to live long enough to take what’s yours?”

She turned her back on him.

The anthem swelled around her, wrapping her in its embrace. The air warmed. The jasmine scent returned, sweet and intoxicating. Her fingers tingled. Her throat ached with the memory of song.

Orsino’s breath was a ragged whisper. “You don’t understand what you’re doing.”

She did.

For the first time, she did.

The Conservatory’s lies were unraveling. The anthem was undoing the Edicts. The silver throne wasn’t a seat.

It was a *voice*.

And it was *hers*.

Orsino’s hands clenched into fists. “I’ll tell Veyla everything. I’ll tell her you’re the heir. I’ll tell her the anthem is yours by blood and not by trickery.”

Sylphine didn’t react.

He took a step closer. “I can protect you. I can hide you. But you have to come *now.*”

She turned her head just enough to meet his gaze.

Then she signed, slow and deliberate.

*You already sold me.*

His face went slack.

The anthem’s climax hit. The courtyard trembled. The mirrors shattered.

Orsino bolted for the Hall of Harmonics, his robes flapping behind him like broken wings.

Sylphine stayed.

She breathed.

The anthem faded into silence.

Then, from the shadows beneath the portico, a figure stepped into the light.

Kaelis Vire removed his hood. His mask was gone. His expression was unreadable.

He held a single sheet of music.

The original draft.

The one Orsino had dropped.

He held out his hand.

Not in invitation.

In warning.

Sylphine didn’t move.

Kaelis’s voice was low. “They’re coming for you.”

She knew.

The Chorus had heard the anthem’s response.

And they wouldn’t stop until they’d sung the song to its end.

chapters.title: **Chapter 9: The Offer**
chapters.text:
**Chapter 9: The Offer**

The slums of Luminar hummed with the reek of damp stone and fried lentils, the alleyway narrow enough that Sylphine’s shoulders brushed both walls as she walked. The morning’s rain had left the cobbles slick, and the light was the color of old parchment—faded, brittle.

She stopped beneath the sagging awning of a shuttered spice shop, where the air smelled of cinnamon and regret. Her fingers traced the edge of the lyre hidden beneath her coat. The silver strings had vibrated in Orsino’s presence, just enough for her to know he’d seen her. And if he’d seen her, others would have too.

A shadow detached itself from the deeper gloom of the alley.

“You’re harder to find than a stolen sonnet,” Orsino said, stepping into the wan light. His scholar’s robe was gone, replaced by a sleeveless tunic and trousers—clothing meant for travel. His spectacles glinted as he adjusted them.

Sylphine didn’t answer. She kept her hands loose at her sides, her stance wide. The lyre’s presence steadied her.

Orsino exhaled through his nose, a sound like a man resigning himself to bad wine. He held up a folded document, edges crisp with official seals. “A pardon. Full reinstatement. Your name restored.”

She watched him. Waited.

He stepped closer. “The Conservatory wants you back, Sylphine. Not as a thief. Not as a fraud. As their prodigy. As *her*.”

The name curled around the last word, like a snake testing its fangs.

Sylphine tilted her head. Just enough to let him see her eyes.

Orsino swallowed. “You don’t have to hide anymore. You don’t have to fight. You compose the anthem, and you take your place at Veyla’s side. As her equal.”

The air between them thickened. Not with heat. With pressure. The lyre’s strings hummed, a low, resonant thrum that only she could hear.

He held out the document. “Sign this. Walk away. Let the anthem belong to the Conservatory again.”

Sylphine stared at the paper. Then at him.

She raised her hands and signed, slow and deliberate:

*I already did.*

Orsino’s fingers twitched. His mouth thinned. “You don’t understand what you’re giving up.”

She signed again, sharper this time:

*I understand better than you.*

He flinched. “The Chorus is close. They’re singing your anthem into the city’s bones. They’ll unravel everything—Veyla’s Edicts, the Conservatory’s lies, the Silver Throne itself. And when they do, they’ll take it. Not for you. For their guild.”

The lyre’s hum grew louder, vibrating against her ribs.

Orsino’s voice dropped. “I can shield you. I can give you a new name, a new life. But only if you walk away from this.”

Sylphine signed, fierce and final:

*I am the anthem.*

The words hung in the damp air like a challenge.

Orsino’s breath came faster. He dropped the pardon into the gutter. It landed in a puddle, the ink bleeding into the filth. “Then you’re already dead.”

She didn’t react. She didn’t have to.

He took a step back. Then another. His hand hovered near the dagger at his belt, but he didn’t draw it. “You’ll regret this.”

Sylphine waited until he turned his back. She watched him disappear into the maze of alleys, his silhouette swallowed by the slums.

Then she exhaled.

The lyre’s hum faded.

She turned toward the Silver Catacombs, a journey that would take her through the Garden of Silent Strings, where she’d been found as a child. The path was dangerous—patrolled by Veyla’s enforcers, haunted by the ghosts of the Vaelith line. But the catacombs held answers. The tombs held verses of the anthem, inscribed on the walls in silver ink that only she could read.

A shadow shifted on the rooftop above.

Sylphine froze.

Kaelis Vire stood outlined against the pale sky, his dark cloak blending with the slate tiles. His mask was gone, leaving his face exposed—sharp cheekbones, a stubborn jaw, eyes that held the weight of secrets.

He didn’t speak. He didn’t move.

She didn’t either.

The silence between them was a living thing, tangled with old promises and newer warnings.

Kaelis tilted his head toward the catacombs. Then he pointed to the ground.

At her shadow.

The sunlight caught the edge of it, and there, on the damp cobbles, the silhouette of the anthem’s first bars shimmered in silver light.

A message.

A claim.

A crown.

chapters.title: **Chapter 10: The Scope**
chapters.text:
**Chapter 10: The Scope**

The Silver Catacombs did not welcome visitors.

Sylphine knew this before she set foot inside. The air smelled of damp stone and old violets, the scent of flowers pressed between the pages of a forgotten book. The entrance was a jagged maw in the cliff face, its edges carved with the same silver filigree as her veins. She ran her palm along the cold metal—each twist of the design hummed under her fingertips, the allegory of the anthem given form.

She stepped over the threshold.

The moment her boot crossed the line, the door behind her groaned shut. Not a latch. Not a lock. Something older. Something that remembered.

Sylphine didn’t turn.

She exhaled. The damp air curled around her like a living thing. She traced the first verse of the anthem on the nearest sarcophagus.

*Where shadows bow to silver light,*
*The lost are found in darkest night.*

The silver ink glowed faintly beneath her fingers. Not like fire. Like memory surfacing through water.

She moved deeper.

The catacombs stretched in perfect silence, a buried cathedral of the dead. Tombs lined the walls, each carved with a verse of the anthem. Some were fresh. Some were centuries old. The oldest bore the name Vaelith in looping script, the letters worn nearly smooth by time.

But the newest one—

*Sylphine Vaelith, Whose Song Shall Bind the Throne.*

The words were bold. Proud. Not the name of a beggar. Not the name of a ghost.

The name of an heir.

She knelt. Pressed her palm to the stone. The verse flared white-hot for two heartbeats, then dimmed to a steady pulse, like a heartbeat.

A voice spoke from the dark.

Not behind her. Not beside her.

*Within* her.

It wasn’t sound. It was resonance. A vibration in the marrow of her bones, in the hollow behind her collarbones, in the silver filaments of her blood.

*“Seraphine.”*

She didn’t flinch. She didn’t speak.

The voice continued, softer now. Familiar, in the way a half-remembered lullaby is familiar.

*“You were never the lost song.”*

Sylphine stood. Turned slowly.

No one was there.

The silence pressed in. The tombs watched. The air hummed with the weight of names—generations of singers, bound in silver thread.

Then, from the far end of the catacombs, a sound.

A step.

Not on stone. On something that *yielded*. Like walking on frozen river water.

Sylphine’s breath stopped.

The step came again.

Closer.

A figure emerged from the gloom—tall, cloaked in black, her silver mask catching the faint glow of the inscribed verses. Liora. Leader of the Chorus of Echoes.

Her gloved fingers brushed a tomb. A verse lit up in the dark.

*Where shadows bow to silver light,*
*The lost are found in darkest night.*

Liora turned. Her mask tilted, as if she were studying a painting from across the room.

“You’ve been singing,” she said. “All this time.”

Sylphine didn’t answer. She folded her arms. Waited.

Liora took another step forward. Her boots made no sound on the stone.

“You didn’t compose the anthem.” Her voice was calm. Almost sad. “You *are* it.”

The words hung in the air like a verdict.

Sylphine exhaled through her nose. One slow breath. Out. In.

Liora tilted her head. “You don’t believe me.”

Silence.

Then—

*“You were never the lost song.”*

The words echoed not from Liora’s lips, but from the tombs themselves. From the silver ink. From the air. From Sylphine’s own ribs.

Liora’s gloved hand twitched. Her mask didn’t move, but Sylphine could feel the shock rippling through her.

The catacomb’s great door groaned again. The sound vibrated through the floor, through Sylphine’s bones. The verses on the walls pulsed, brighter now, as if waking from a long sleep.

Liora’s voice cut through the resonance, sharp and uncertain.

“Wait.”

Sylphine didn’t.

She turned and ran toward the back of the catacombs, where a narrow stair descended into deeper dark. The glow of the verses followed her, a silver tide.

Behind her, Liora’s breath hitched. A curse. A footstep.

Then—

The door slammed shut.

Not just closed. *Sealed.*

A finality like a guillotine’s fall.

Sylphine skidded to a stop. Turned.

The door was gone. In its place, a seamless wall of silver-inlaid stone. The last verse of the anthem—one she didn’t recognize—flared to life across its surface.

*When the singer claims the unseen throne,*
*The song shall rise, the lies be known.*

She reached out. Her fingers hovered over the inscription.

Liora’s voice echoed through the chamber, muffled as if through water.

*“You were never the lost song.”*

Then, softer:

*“You were the singer.”*

The verse dimmed.

The air went still.

Sylphine lowered her hand.

She was alone.

And for the first time in her life, she wasn’t hiding.

She was starting.

Chapter 11

The Hall of Harmonics smelled of polished oak and old sweat.

Sylphine stood at the edge of the grand gallery, fingers curled around the balustrade. Below, the Conservatory’s elite milled like moths around a dying flame—patrons whispering, scribes scribbling, and at the center, Maestros Veyla, her back to the room, conducting a conversation with a cluster of aging baritones. The air hummed with tension, not music. The silence between notes was louder than any choral swell.

She adjusted the stolen epaulet on her shoulder. The silver thread matched the filigree on her wrist, the same pattern as the earliest verses in the Silver Catacombs. A reminder. A warning.

Footsteps clicked behind her.

Orsino.

He paused a half-step too far away, as if fearing the echo of proximity. His coat was too tight for the man who once filled it with swagger.

“You’re not safe here,” he said.

Sylphine didn’t turn. “I never was.”

“Veyla knows. The notes—”

“They’re not notes. They’re bloodlines.” She tapped the railing. The wood thrummed under her fingers, barely audible over the rustle of parchment and the murmur of voices. “You should have burned the manuscript when you had the chance.”

Orsino exhaled through his nose. “I tried. The ink won’t burn.”

“Of course it won’t.” She finally looked at him. His face was thinner than she remembered, his eyes shadowed beneath the ledgers he’d once claimed to love. “Vaelith ink remembers. It waits.”

A tremor ran through the room. Not from the floor. From the air itself—a ripple like a plucked string, lingering in the silence.

Orsino’s throat worked. “She’s losing them.”

Sylphine followed his gaze to the back of the hall, where Maestros Veyla stood rigid, her fingers twitching as if plucking phantom strings. A patron turned away mid-conversation, his voice cutting off mid-sentence. Another wiped his brow, then left without a word.

The Conservatory was unraveling.

Orsino leaned closer, voice low. “The anthem isn’t hers. It’s yours. And it’s changing.”

“I know.”

“You *feel* it, don’t you? In your blood?”

A laugh escaped her, sharp and humorless. “That’s all I’ve felt for years.”

Before he could respond, a servant in black livery pushed through the crowd, his face pale. He dropped to one knee before Veyla, breath ragged.

“Maestros.” His voice cracked. “The Silver Throne—”

Veyla’s hand shot out, gripping his collar. “Speak.”

“It—it *glows* when she touches it.”

Silence. Then:

Veyla released him. The servant fell back, scrambling away.

Sylphine didn’t move. But her fingers tightened on the balustrade until her knuckles ached.

Veyla turned slowly.

Their eyes met across the hall.

The maestro’s face was a map of fractures—rage, denial, dawning comprehension. Her lips parted. No sound came out.

Then the chandelier above them dimmed, as if something had siphoned the light. The silver threads in the air—always present, always dismissed as decoration—shimmered like spider silk in a draft.

Sylphine exhaled. Finally, she spoke.

“You’ve been singing my song.”

Veyla’s voice cracked like ice. “No.”

“Yes.” Sylphine stepped forward, her boots clicking on the polished floor. “You borrowed it. You shaped it. You made it *hers*.”

“It’s mine by right of craft. By devotion. By *blood* earned.” Veyla’s voice rose, strident. “I took you from the slums. I forged you.”

“You forged a ghost.” Sylphine’s hand touched the silver epaulet again. “And now the ghost is walking.”

Veyla’s jaw clenched. “Prove it.”

Sylphine did not hesitate. She strode toward the center of the hall, where the Silver Throne waited—an ornate chair carved with the anthem’s verses, its metal cool to the touch. She placed two fingers on the armrest.

The throne flared white.

Not a reflection. Not an illusion.

A resonance.

The Conservatory gasped. Some crossed themselves. A few patrons scrambled for the exits.

Veyla’s breath came in shallow bursts. “That’s impossible.”

“Nothing is impossible,” Sylphine said, still facing the throne. “Only hidden.”

She closed her eyes. Not to hide. To listen.

The throne sang back.

A melody, faint but unmistakable, threaded through the silence. A verse Sylphine had never heard aloud, only felt in her marrow. A verse that belonged to her.

*Where shadows bow to silver light,*
*The lost are found in darkest night.*

Veyla’s voice cut through the resonance like a blade. “Stop it.”

Sylphine opened her eyes. The throne’s glow pulsed in time with her heartbeat.

Veyla advanced, her skirts swishing against the floor. “You’re not a Vaelith. You’re a fraud. A fabrication. A—”

“A lie you taught me to sing.” Sylphine turned. The throne’s light spilled across her face, silver and harsh. “Look at me, Maestros. Really look.”

Veyla did.

For the first time, she saw the silver in Sylphine’s veins. Not the stain of ink. The glimmer of lineage. The radiance of a bloodline that had slumbered for centuries.

Veyla’s hand flew to her mouth.

A patron nearby retched.

Then, from the back of the hall, a new sound.

A single, clear note.

Pure. Resonant.

Unmistakable.

Sylphine’s breath stopped.

No one else seemed to hear it. Or if they did, they didn’t react. But she felt it in her teeth, in her ribs, in the marrow of her bones.

The anthem.

Not as a fragment. Not as a ghost.

As a *command*.

Veyla’s eyes widened. “What was that?”

Sylphine didn’t answer.

She knew what it was.

And she knew, with a certainty that hollowed her chest, that it had been sung for her.

For the first time in her life, she wasn’t the one being chased.

She was the one being summoned.

Chapter 12

The Hall of Harmonics had never been so quiet.

The elite of the Conservatory stood frozen in clusters, like reeds in a sudden stillness, their breath shallow, their eyes darting between Sylphine and Maestros Veyla. The chandeliers hung heavy, their crystals dimmed to pewter. The silver threads woven through the air—always dismissed as mere ornament—now shimmered with purpose, pulsing like nerves.

Sylphine didn’t look at them.

She looked at Veyla.

The maestro’s face had gone the color of old parchment. Her lips trembled. She gripped the podium like it was the only thing anchoring her to the floor.

“You lied,” Sylphine said.

Veyla’s fingers twitched. “No.”

“You took a melody that wasn’t yours.” Sylphine stepped forward. The silver epaulet at her shoulder caught the dim light, glinting like a blade. “You shaped it. You polished it. You made the world believe it was *yours*.”

“It *is* mine,” Veyla snapped. “By fifteen years of devotion. By blood poured into rehearsal. By sacrifices no one sees!”

A ripple passed through the room. A scribe dropped his pen. A patron’s fan slid from limp fingers.

Sylphine didn’t flinch. “You didn’t compose it.”

“Then who did?” Veyla’s voice cracked. “Who?”

Sylphine lifted her hand.

From her sleeve, a scrap of parchment unfurled—crumpled, creased, glowing faintly in the low light. Orsino’s voice rose from the back of the hall.

“No—”

Too late.

Sylphine let the parchment drift to the floor. It didn’t flutter. It *hovered*, suspended an inch above the boards, as if held by an unseen hand.

The Conservatory’s breath hitched.

Then, the parchment began to *sing*.

Not with voice. Not with instrument.

With *silence*.

A tone so pure it wasn’t heard—it was *felt*. In the teeth. In the spine. In the marrow of every listener. The silver threads in the air vibrated in perfect unison, plucked by an invisible bow. The chandeliers chimed in minor harmonics. A goblet on a distant table shattered into crystalline fragments.

Veyla’s hands went to her ears—though no sound touched them.

“Stop it,” she rasped.

Sylphine didn’t move. “You sang my anthem for fifteen years. You bound a dynasty to my song. You made the Conservatory kneel to a melody that wasn’t yours to claim.”

A ghost.

It was singing *Sylphine’s*.
